speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three with Sonia Friedman. So this woman gave birth to a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave birth to a little boy. And I don't remember who decided, but they decided that we'll go somewhere closer. That we will not go as far as-- and we went to town Penza. We didn't stop in Penza. It was like industrial city, quite big. They put us on a horse and buggy. We came to a small town, and they told us, this is not it. We went farther. We came to a village. I told that I'm reading one of the classics, Russian classics from 200 years before. It was a stark-looking place with wooden houses and one street, just like raw wood and nothing else. And it used to get dark real early. We came and they put us to live with an old woman. And their family with somebody else. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The whole town was only a collective farm. The people that worked on the collective farm lived there. There was one building that was the office of the collective farm. So it was decided that I and the girl my-- from the other family will go into the small town and see if we can get some jobs, and maybe we can move there. Because I don't remember how, but I learned that the commissioner that the Russians sent to Rovno, a Russian woman, is now in this town, in this small town. And we came there. We walked like 20 kilometers. And we came here, I got this very fashionable European clothes, and my shoes are falling apart. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Told us where she lives. I went to her, and she says, gladly, she'll give me a job for the-- in malaria station. Malaria was very bad there. They had to have a dispensation place, and statistics for a malaria station. She give me the job, and the other girl she gave a job something like typing somewhere else. Put us with a family, with a woman, had five children. And one was not a crip, but you know, like a swing, a wooden swing. It was hanging from the ceiling. And the baby-- the kids cried constantly. They were hungry. And the woman was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she was just praying that the baby should starve. And we had one time a day some kind of a soup where we were going. And it was depressing. We were lonely. This place looked so-- the whole place looked so strange, barn-like, like somewhere on the other end of the world. And we decided to walk home. We decided, we'll see, we'll walk on for the weekend and see. We didn't know. We didn't have mail. There was no telephone, nothing. When I came back, which my mother tells me that the [RUSSIAN], the director of the farm, learned that I'm a bookkeeper and they need a bookkeeper. And he wants me to go and work in the office. So I get this job in the office now. The bookkeeping is not done like it's done-- because they don't have money. They-- the unit of payment was after the whole season, whatever the-- they pay down from the fields and divide it. I mean, it's quite a complete different bookkeeping anyway. But they gave me the job. And he said that he'll help me, whatever he can. You know, with some wood, because the old woman-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't remember if we changed places, but she only wanted me to ask because I work in the office and I'll get wood to warm the house. All the time I couldn't believe that this is real. There was a 15 years old boy there, ugly guy looking. If he lived, survived, he probably would've been just like Hitler. A sadist, smart, like a whip. He knew everything. And the people now in the whole village, there were the two of us families, and only the member of another family from Russia, from [NON-ENGLISH] who had one son who was a little retarded adult. And he wasn't taken, because everybody else was taken into the army. And-- but in the office was a young woman who was-- who came back to her in-laws because she had a baby, and her husband was in the army. And she was in Rovno the two years when it was next to Russia. And she was very sweet and very nice. And we became good friends. She invited me to her house, and they lived very good. She had very nice silvers. They were well to do. They-- they had underground everything, food and everything else. And they told me this story, and it was dressed like a real, prosperous [NON-ENGLISH], those were the landowners. And they say that-- I said, I'm surprised that the Communist didn't do anything to them. He says because his older son was the first to go in the church and break the top, destroy the religion in the village. So they respected him. Make a long story short, the other family didn't have anybody employed. What we were getting was-- yeah, now this guy, the [NON-ENGLISH] was mostly drunk. And he had a girlfriend, probably could be his daughter. And you couldn't find him never. And to get a order for something to get from them, the villagers only used to go to work because they were wearing like very warm-- make like a-- filled with cotton, like jackets. And inside they had very big pockets. So whatever they stole going home, that was the pay for the day because all the threads didn't amount to anything. I don't know before the war, but during the war, because they had to give away the grain to the army, so they had to go to work, so that's what they used to steal, whatever they could they took down from the fields. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] now the other family didn't have anything to-- she had the child who was-- she couldn't feed him, because she had no milk. But he was very cute. He lives in Israel. He was a cute baby. And an old woman just was giving milk, and-- but her father had ulcers, and he was really suffering from hunger. So he came to the office one time. And he waited for days so that the director would give him an order for some food, for some grain, or for something. And finally he found him. And he gave him the order. And before he leaves, this young guy says, you-- you need my signature, too. And the director already left. He gives him the paper, and he tears it to pieces, and then throws it out. The end was that he was going to beg. He was a musician. He played the violin. He was going-- he was going to beg by the-- like a little out of town and beg. And one time they found him frozen to death, sitting there. And the villagers wouldn't bury him because the ground was so hard that they used to keep them till they had about 10 and then dig a hole. It was-- at 12 o'clock it used to get dark, at four o'clock, it was pitch-- I mean, even after 12 o'clock, because there was-- everything was closed. There was no mail, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the postmaster of the village approached me. She says if I could give lessons to her daughter, she said she'll try to help us with the food. So I went through like fields, and I heard wolves howling. And each time-- yeah, so my-- the old woman that I lived was so impressed that I'm a teacher, I work in the office, she gave me a woolen boots to wear. So each time I put it in, I had to work real hard to pull this one out and the other one fell in.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, it didn't work. I went there a few times and I couldn't-- I mean, I couldn't get there. And she couldn't get out of there. So nothing happened. So we lived there for nine months. And we couldn't wait till it will start-- the snow would start thawing. In May it started to thaw. Now while I was there in the office, at one time there's this boy came-- I mean he was harassing me all the time, sexually, stalking me out. And he was 15 years old. I was 19. I was almost-- I was 20 already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- he came to call me. He says somebody wants to see you. I said, who wants to see me? I'm not going at night. So he says there is-- I'm not kidding, he says. The [NON-ENGLISH] wants to see you. So I came outside. There's two healthy horses with a four covered thing. And I come in this very handsome officer. I didn't know what's [NON-ENGLISH], what's not [NON-ENGLISH] He was very polite. He took my heavy hair coat and gave me a chair. And I said-- and he asked me about the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] refugees that live here, and so on. And he said that, yeah-- what he had was a German note. They took it from a-- it was a godforsaken place, even the town. He said they took it from a German from a POW. And can I translate it for him? And I translated it. It was a very simple note. And he said, if you ever to come to town or you need something to help you, he gave me his address. So when the snow started to melt, we started to walk toward this town. The water was-- I mean, I still have clothes, but I had a pair of army boots, which is another story with the army. The army was one time there. Do I have time to tell you about it? One evening they say that a-- a battalion of military workers are passing by, and that the village has to accommodate them. And they brought news about the war, the-- the Germans are near Stalingrad, and the Russian army's in the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: I come over to-- I'm come off the work to this room where we live, that was full with military men. And they put some officers there. I couldn't find a place where to sleep, because I used to sleep probably on the floor, I don't remember. But I remember that night sleeping on the floor. So I look all over. I see one empty space. They had a-- a cellar from the kitchen now, so it's very cold. So nobody laid there. Probably I laid down, and I hear snoring, I see feet, army soldiers. I wait till the morning and want to know what it is. So the oldest of them introduces himself, that it's a battalion of them. And they're on the way there and there. And as the days went by, it seems like they didn't want to move. They said, unless they give them shoes and protect them from the cold, they're not moving. And they were negotiating this thing. And they were staying there. They had horses. And they had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they were all goyim. Never had an idea of what it means, Jewish. One even wanted me to get married with him. And you should have seen him, I mean, a Cossack or something like that. I mean, even the way they were flirting was so strange, was so-- was so different. Anyway, they left. But he was the one that told them no shoes, no-- we're not going to dig. They had to dig for transportation, new places. Anyway, we came to this town. And I-- I go and knock on the door of that address I have. It's a nice building, I mean, it's a small town. Come in, Persian carpets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful furniture. That was the small town that we passed on the way going to the village. Oh, not the old-- one time being in the office, a Jew comes in with a-- with a-- with a horse and buggy. He says he lives there. He is from Poland. He was in Rovno. He's a-- he's a refugee just like us. He lives there. What he's doing here, he's going to the woods to cut some wood, by himself. And he saw we didn't have shoes. I said, the next time I come, I bring you-- my wife will make you some shoes. And his wife bought a whole sheep coat, and she made us shoes with it and he came this. Now he told me, when you get-- when the snow melts, come back to town. And so first I went to-- to this-- because you had to have a permit to move from one place to another one. We couldn't leave the village on our own. So when I came to this town, and I knocked on the door, and I saw all the opulence inside of the building. And this, I hadn't seen it since I was traveling from Ukraine. So he probably was very, very big here. And he said that he assign me some housing. Then we found his family, he and his wife. And we became close friends, till California. That was a long way from that-- from that village in Russia. And we moved there. We moved there. He was in Poland. This man, he gave my father a job. And in the beginning I was working there. He was in Poland. He had in [NON-ENGLISH] a factory to dry foods that they used to export to Germany, prunes, and apples, and he knew how to do this. All the time being there he kept saying I should have stayed with the Germans. I don't believe that they're doing all those things. They would have-- they would need me, and I would probably make money with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father worked there. And I worked there for a while in the office. Now, he was working in the warehouse. And in the office was such an anti-Semitism. The only Jews were-- I was in the office. The president of the company was a Russian Jew named Gitlen, and they used to all the time put his name Hitler. They used to pretend that they're making a mistake.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and he was in the warehouse, so did my father work in the warehouse. The people that worked in the office were all hungry. If you came in the morning and you went to have a drink of water, they used to say, only if you have a good breakfast you want to drink. They were like hollow-faced. There was a intelligent woman. She was always-- she had a son. She was always hungry. The woman that used to take care, cleaning, she was always hungry. There was another-- was what-- another bookkeeper-- well, that's the office policies. But that's not important. One thing I want to recall is while I was there I make friends with that woman that was-- she was very intelligent. She knew Polish. She knew literature, she knew poetry. She was very intelligent, but she was a mean look about her. One time she tells me, you know, my husband is on vacation here. We want to invite you for lunch, for dinner. I don't remember. I came to her and I met her husband, tall, handsome officer. On the table was everything I hadn't seen since the Russians came to Rovno. There were caviar, conserves of chicken, the wine, and bread, and butter. Anyway, I think I ate, and they kept telling me, eat, eat, eat, eat. She said, her husband brought everything with him. Was a very intelligent man, very well read. And I spent a very nice time. They took me almost halfway to the-- and he left tomorrow. He left back to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was when I was working in that [NON-ENGLISH]. Now they send me to the big city for a seminar in statistics. Plans and statistics. When I came back with the knowledge, they made me head of the department. And I thought I've got a heart attack there, because all the statistics that I was giving, were lies. They were not harvesting so much. They weren't sending so much. And when I used to tell this president, he says Sonia, why do you worry? My name is above yours. So I said, [NON-ENGLISH], and it's terrific news that I'll be sitting with you in Serbia. He had a Jewish wife. Gitlen was a middle-aged man, she looked something like an old Jewish lady. And he had a girlfriend who was like dynamite somewhere in the village. He had to have a truck driver's mouth.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he saved my life, not one time, but more than once. They kept calling me to that to me to the army. I used to get notes to go to the army. Sent notes to the army, you know, leave it to my parents. They said, as long as we here, we're not dividing. Meanwhile, my parents were looking-- the war was already-- when the war started-- well, that was later, anyway. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And between-- one time they really call me to the army. My sister, meanwhile, finished nursing. And she was working for the-- the military hospital. She was a-- a specialist in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] x-- not on x-ray. Anyway, they used to bring the transports with thOSE butchered up-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when they-- I was getting there, and she had a bus, was a military man, a Jew, a Russian Jew with this wife. And she introduced me to them. And I told them to once about the army. And he said, you know, if you worked here, you would have [NON-ENGLISH], which is exemption from the army, because it's a military place. But you have to be a member of the party. I can't take you to statistics here. And I said, well, I'm not a member in the party. Well anyway, I got a job, and I was the only one that ever worked in this department because we were getting how many cripples they have and everything. And I was working just with one older woman that she was is this apartment. So meanwhile I was safe from the army. And then-- then the hospital-- when the war started to get better for the Russians, the hospital had to go up front. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, that's not the end. I get-- I get a letter when the water started to go good for the Russians. And the town from where I came, Rovno, was liberated. I get a notice from the [NON-ENGLISH], which means from the army. I have a choice. Or I go in the army or I go back and work in the post, in the division from the telegraphs. Of course, I picked this rather than who wanted to go back anyway, as soon as it's possible. So I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] yeah, that was-- but before that, one time this Gitlen, I get a note of the army, and it's no joke. All my Russian friends are in the army already. Some of them are already killed. I get a notice. I wasn't even home. And my mother accepted the summons, so you can't get out of it, and it's a weekend. That on Monday I should come bringing-- bring a cup with all the things they tell you. And the guy, my-- my director from the organization is in the village there with his girlfriend. And I walked there, eight miles. And they were in the-- they used to have those steam baths underground. But there was a little window. I knock on the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, they calling me back to the army. He says, don't go. So I said, what do you mean, don't go? They'll shoot me. Well anyway, he went back with me. He went to the-- vodka, and fruit, and cabbage. Send it to there, and he said, don't show up. Anyway, I got, again, out of the army. Then I got a notice that I have to go back or here. I had to go to the big city and get my passport stamped. And the war was still going on. And then I had to go back. And my parents are staying there. We are in a godforsaken place, so far from anything. But you know, that family that I told you that they came with a horse and buggy and brought me the shoes, he says he's going there, that that was drying fruits in Poland. He says he's going, too. I said, what do you mean you're going, too? He says for money I can get a paper. He shows up at the train and he goes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we travel through destroyed-- everything in Russia was destroyed, the bridges, the trains. We were crossing a train from Kiev. It used to be a huge bridge that went all through town. It was a temporary something. It was shaking like anything. There were no lights because there were blackouts. And my sister was-- she was a military nurse. She was wearing a custom made-- she's very little. So she had a custom made military coat, and the hat, the whole thing. So she gave me to wear it because I didn't have a coat. So she gave me the coat and the whole thing to wear. We were in a car, in a passenger's car, driving at night full with soldiers, and they all drunk. And such a poison, anti-Semitic poison spits from their mouths all night. That the Jews are fighting the war. And Uzbekistan and [NON-ENGLISH], that they getting rich on this speculeering-- speculations. I mean, I can't even repeat. I mean, I was afraid. I thought we should jump the train. Jump in the water, because they'll kill us in the morning. And they-- and they don't even know if I'm a girl, because I just look like them. And I have my hat. In the morning was-- when it got light, we had to go and get some food. So we stopped somewhere. And like nothing, didn't say a bad word. They asked me if they can do this with me, can do something else with me. But that stayed with us out there because that's when they were themselves, driving-- going on this train. And we came to Rovno, from the city where we started out.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1944. The city was just liberated. The first thing I pass by where I lived. But when you go with the main street, those big houses, like real big city, you have to go in in a gate, go through the-- go through this old court. And then you go out to a private street, and then the houses. Now, the whole court was destroyed. You could see from the street-- this-- from the main street, you could see the little street with my house. And it probably was shook up by a bomb because there's some metal like braced.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we knocked on the door, both of us. Nobody answered. We went to second, the third, the fourth door. An old woman says, what are you doing here? You're alive? She says, run from the city. They're bombarding. All my children don't-- nobody's here. They're bombarding the city every night. Go under-- at the end of town. So we went to the end of town. There were a lot more people there. And in a barn we stayed. And they came with their aeroplanes, I never had it. I started to shake. I could shake like that for four, five hours without stopping, you couldn't stop me. I don't know, I didn't react like that in the beginning. I didn't have this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few days it started to get better. I came back to the office, and I meet the same guy who took me on the truck. And I say, listen, I have to make papers for my parents to come here. He says, I'll help you. And he was not drunk. He was a wonderful person. But most of time he was drunk. And I started to-- I don't remember if I worked, or I just got-- and I saw the first Jews that came out from partisans, and that lived in a group. I didn't see anybody from camps or anything like that. And then they made us-- that's when I came for good, and they made us look when they hung up those Ukrainians. That's when I-- yeah, that's when I came the second time. So and they made out papers for me. Took a month. And meanwhile I met, in one house, a lot of partisans that were in the woods then. And they lived about 20 people together. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So once we made the papers, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had to go on a train. It took us a month to get back, you know.
